mitre_attack: T1499.001 - Endpoint Denial of Service: OS Exhaustion Flood
T1498.001 - Network Denial of Service: Direct Network Flood
cwe: CWE-770 - Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling
CWE-404 - Improper Resource Shutdown or Release

threat.title: Database Connection Pool Exhaustion via Slowloris-Style Attack
threat.description: Attackers can exhaust database connection pools by opening numerous slow or incomplete database queries that hold connections indefinitely, preventing legitimate users from accessing the application. This attack is similar to Slowloris HTTP attacks but targets database connection pools, causing cascading failures across application layers.

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker identifies application endpoints that trigger database queries without proper timeout limits
2. Opens 50-100 concurrent connections to the application, each executing slow SELECT queries with complex JOINs or sleeps
3. Each query holds a database connection from the pool for 5-10 minutes
4. Connection pool (default 10-20 connections) becomes fully saturated within seconds
5. Legitimate user requests begin timing out with 'Connection pool exhausted' errors
6. Application becomes completely unavailable as all incoming requests queue indefinitely waiting for connections
prerequisites: Application uses connection pooling with default or low maximum connection limits
No connection timeout or query timeout configured
No rate limiting on database-heavy endpoints
Application does not properly release connections after errors

cve_references.description: PostgreSQL connection pooler MITM vulnerability affecting PgBouncer, PgPool-II, and Odyssey that can be exploited for DoS attacks

real_world_breaches.incident: Production Web Application Connection Pool Exhaustion
real_world_breaches.impact: 3.5-hour outage affecting 80% of users with HTTP 500 errors; revenue loss estimated at $250,000
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Database connection pool exhaustion due to misconfigured HikariCP settings and connection leaks from long-running transactions

real_world_breaches.impact: Multiple production server crashes over 2-week period; complete service unavailability during crashes
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Connection pool running out of connections with 'request timed out after 30002ms' errors due to connection adder thread becoming stuck

real_world_breaches.impact: Database connection pool exhaustion under high concurrency preventing workflow execution
real_world_breaches.root_cause: High concurrency workflows exhausting available database connections without proper pool sizing

security_research.title: Connection Pool Exhaustion: The Silent Killer
security_research.key_finding: Connection pool exhaustion is described as 'the most insidious failure in distributed systems because it looks invisible until it destroys everything'

security_research.title: Diagnosing Connection Leaks in Node.js and Postgres
security_research.key_finding: Connection leaks in Node.js pg pools cause applications to become unresponsive once the maximum connection limit is reached (default 10 connections)

security_research.title: Detection of Slowloris Attacks using Machine Learning Algorithms
security_research.key_finding: Random forest ML algorithms can detect Slowloris-style attacks (which can target connection pools) with 99.52% accuracy

mitigations.title: Configure Connection Pool Limits with Adaptive Sizing
mitigations.description: Implement proper connection pool sizing based on application load patterns, database capacity, and expected concurrency. Use formula: connections = ((core_count * 2) + effective_spindle_count) for optimal performance.
iso27001_controls: A.17.2.1 - Availability of information processing facilities
nist_csf: PR.DS-4 - Adequate capacity to ensure availability
DE.AE-5 - Incident alert thresholds are established
validation_steps: Verify max pool size is set appropriately (typically 10-30 connections)
Confirm connection and query timeouts are configured
Test pool behavior under load with 2x expected concurrent users
Monitor pool metrics (total, idle, waiting connections)
Verify connections are released in all code paths (including errors)

mitigations.title: Implement Connection Timeout and Query Timeout Guards
mitigations.description: Configure aggressive timeouts at multiple layers (application, connection pool, database) to prevent slow queries from holding connections indefinitely. Use HikariCP for production Java applications with battle-tested timeout handling.
nist_csf: PR.IP-1 - A baseline configuration is created and maintained
validation_steps: Set leakDetectionThreshold to 60000ms (1 minute) in development
Test long-running query scenarios to verify timeout enforcement
Monitor HikariCP pool metrics via JMX or Micrometer
Implement circuit breaker pattern for database calls
Verify graceful degradation when pool is exhausted

mitigations.title: Enable Connection Leak Detection and Monitoring
mitigations.description: Implement comprehensive monitoring and alerting for connection pool health, including leak detection, pool saturation alerts, and query performance tracking. Use SIEM queries to detect anomalous connection patterns.
nist_csf: DE.AE-3 - Event data are aggregated and correlated
validation_steps: Verify connection leak detection alerts trigger after 60 seconds
Confirm Prometheus metrics are exported on /metrics endpoint
Set up Grafana dashboard for pool health visualization
Test alerting system with simulated connection leak
Review SIEM queries detect anomalous connection patterns

indicators: Sudden spike in database connection count approaching max_connections
Increased application response times with 'connection timeout' errors
High number of connections in 'idle in transaction' state
Multiple connections from same IP address or user session
Query execution times exceeding configured timeouts
Connection pool waiting queue growing continuously
Database CPU usage low while connection count is high

log_queries.query:
// Detect connection pool exhaustion patterns
event.category: "database" AND 
(
  error.message: "connection timeout" OR 
  error.message: "pool exhausted" OR 
  error.message: "Connection is not available"
) 
| stats count by source.ip, user.name
| where count > 10

index=app_logs sourcetype=postgresql
("too many connections" OR "connection pool exhausted" OR "connection timeout")
| stats count by src_ip, db_user, query_duration
| where count > 5 OR query_duration > 30000
| sort -count

// Monitor PostgreSQL connection pool metrics
AzureDiagnostics
| where ResourceType == "POSTGRESQL SERVERS"
| where MetricName in ("active_connections", "connections_failed")
| summarize 
    AvgConnections = avg(Average),
    MaxConnections = max(Maximum),
    FailedConnections = sum(Total)
    by bin(TimeGenerated, 5m), Resource
| where AvgConnections > 80 or FailedConnections > 20
| order by TimeGenerated desc

-- Identify connections in 'idle in transaction' state
SELECT 
  pid,
  usename,
  application_name,
  client_addr,
  state,
  state_change,
  NOW() - state_change AS idle_duration,
  query
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE state = 'idle in transaction'
  AND NOW() - state_change > interval '1 minute'
ORDER BY idle_duration DESC;

-- Monitor connection pool usage and waiting threads
SELECT 
  processlist_user,
  processlist_host,
  processlist_db,
  processlist_command,
  processlist_state,
  COUNT(*) as connection_count,
  SUM(processlist_time) as total_time_seconds
FROM performance_schema.threads
WHERE processlist_command IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY processlist_user, processlist_host, processlist_state
HAVING connection_count > 5
ORDER BY connection_count DESC;

tools.name: pg_stat_statements
tools.usage: SELECT query, calls, mean_exec_time, max_exec_time FROM pg_stat_statements ORDER BY mean_exec_time DESC LIMIT 20;

tools.name: HikariCP JMX Metrics
tools.usage: Monitor via JConsole or Micrometer: hikaricp.connections.active, hikaricp.connections.idle, hikaricp.connections.pending
